subsections.title: Want to learn more?
We hope you enjoy this
McGraw-Hill eBook! If you’d like more information about this book, its author, or related books and websites, please click here.
STRATEGIC SUPPLY
CHAIN MANAGEMENT
The Five Disciplines for Top Performance
Eli Lilly Profile:
Supporting Product
Lifecycles with Supply
Chain Management
Lilly spends about 19 percent of its annual revenue on R&D.
To deal with its growth, Lilly is doubling its production facilities from 20 to 40, largely by using contract manufacturers.
Lilly focuses its supply chain management efforts on never missing a sale—and only secondarily on keeping inventory levels low.
Each new drug being developed fits within an established “toolkit” environment.
A supply chain steward acts as a liaison between the product team and the supply chain.
Core Discipline 1:
View Your Supply Chain as a Strategic Asset
Strategic supply chain management is more than just innovation for the sake of being innovative. It’s creating a unique supply chain configuration that drives forward your strategic objectives.
Consider outsourcing activities with low strategic importance or that a third party could do better, faster, or more cheaply.
If your product or process technology is a source of differentiation, don’t outsource that aspect of your operations.
Companies whose primary strategy is innovation focus on developing category killers—“must have” products that benefit from significant consumer pull.
One key supply chain attribute relative to quality is traceability —the ability to trace a product back to its point of origin—a growing requirement in a number of industries.
A good supply chain strategy is grounded in an understanding of your power and influence relative to customers and suppliers.
Your supply chain strategy, just like your business strategy, has to adapt.
Autoliv Profile:
Applying Rocket Science to the Supply Chain
By 1998, Autoliv realized that it had to cut costs without eroding the quality of its products. “Our goal is to minimize what’s special and increase what’s standardized,” says
Norm Markert, president of Autoliv
North America.
Autoliv’s supply base represents over 60 percent of the total manufacturing costs for its products.
It took Autoliv nearly ten years to fully transform its manufacturing and supply systems.
Core Discipline 2:
Develop an End-to-End
Process Architecture
It’s important before deciding how you want to operate to think critically about why you need to operate a certain way.
We caution you to avoid the trap of choosing costly leading-edge practices that provide only marginal support.
Without shared objectives, it doesn’t make sense to invest in end-to-end processes.
Inconsistency can result from acquisitions, but more typically it arises because companies are simply not aware of the benefits of a standard process architecture.
Complexity is the single greatest cause of poor investment returns in supply chain management.
Your supply chain architecture should describe the construct of your processes and how they interact.
The SCOR model provides a supply chain scorecard for setting and managing supply chain performance targets across the organization.
In the next generation of process architecture, companies will be able to rapidly translate strategies into new supply chains.
Avon Profile:
Calling on Customers
Cost-Effectively
In most European markets, Avon begins a new sales campaign every three weeks.
Avon put in place a regional planning group empowered to make decisions about service levels, inventory, and costs.
Avon created a centralized inventory hub in Poland to service the company’s branches throughout Europe.
Avon is also working to standardize its containers and postpone labeling them to cut costs and increase efficiency.
Avon had to change its own approach so that suppliers could manufacture its products more cost- effectively.
Core Discipline 3:
Design Your Organization for Performance
Your organizational design should not be static—it should evolve with your company.
The goal of an effective supply chain organization is to optimize the end-to-end order- fulfillment process— not individual functions—all while achieving the lowest total cost.
The development of supply chain management as a core competency and an organizational imperative doesn’t just happen—a conscious effort is needed.
Clearly, balancing the competencies you need with the competencies you have is critical to executing your strategy.
As you design your organization, keep in mind that technologies don’t deliver success— people do.
Owens Corning Profile:
Reorganizing for “a Bright Future”
A new SAP system eliminated 500 legacy systems and achieved one of OC’s prevailing goals: to keep it “simple, common, and global.”

OC wanted to see a $250 million improvement in income from operations (IFO) over three years. “We needed to change our mind-set from manufacturing efficiency to supply chain flexibility,” says Sue Hatfield.
OC has recognized the value in combining the technology and supply chain process functions in one organization. “We want to be our customers’ best supplier and our suppliers’ best customer,” says
David Johns.
Core Discipline 4:
Build the Right
Collaborative Model
True collaboration is very difficult, and there’s no point in doing it unless you can achieve financial or strategic gain.
Cooperative relationships have a higher level of information sharing.
Coordinated collaboration requires a high level of negotiation and compromise.
In a synchronized relationship, information is developed jointly rather than just transmitted or exchanged.
A key requirement of effective collaboration is shared metrics, but all too often these are missing.
Today’s collaboration tools focus on supply chain event management and on relationships between customers and suppliers.
Collaboration isn’t about shifting costs from one supply chain partner to another. It’s about setting up the supply chain to lower overall costs and then sharing the savings.
In the next generation of supply chain collaboration, technology advances will be overshadowed by changes in individual attitudes.
U.S. Department of
Defense Profile:
Making the Tail Smaller and the Tooth Stronger
Because of the scope and speed required to transform the military to a higher state of readiness, tomorrow’s defense supply chain will be very different from yesterday’s.
Today, the “tail” that supports the “teeth” of the military is drawing on best practices from private industry.
With its nearly $25 billion in sales and services for fiscal year 2003, the DLA would occupy spot no. 65 on the Fortune 500.
Right now, the DoD is building the partnerships, protocols, and systems that will get us to the goal of end- to-end capability, says Diane Morales, who launched the
FLE initiative. “You don’t stuff a process architecture down the throat of an $80 billion supply chain,” says Laura
Faught. “It’s not about end- to-end distribution but about end-to-end supply, ” says Alan
Estevez.
Core Discipline 5:
Use Metrics to Drive
Business Success
Measurement is the only way to understand whether process performance is improving or worsening and whether action is required.
If you want to pursue balanced objectives, you need to cover multiple performance perspectives and then select your metrics accordingly.
Measurements are only useful to the degree that they enable timely decision making.

Since a supply chain strategy is based on a company’s overall strategic direction and core competencies, you’ll need to choose carefully the metrics that make sense as signals of performance to your objectives.
The metrics embedded in the
SCOR model are consistent with the premise of the supply chain as an end-to-end process.
General Motors Profile:
Driving Customer
Satisfaction
A Roadmap to Change
There is a strong correlation between supply chain maturity and superior performance.
In the next generation, applications will be far better aligned with what companies want and need.
Your strategy always should drive your roadmap, not the other way around.
You need to fix what isn’t working today before you can focus on taking major steps toward next- generation performance.
Look beyond any technical solutions and consider the “softer” factors of your culture and environment.
Your supply chain design should incorporate the next-generation characteristics of transparency, flexibility, and simultaneity.
Poor communication is often the culprit when roadmap initiatives don’t progress according to plan.
Seagate
Technology Profile:
Real-Time Response to Demand
Seagate produces 15 to 20 million disc drives a quarter using 65 million components a day.
Seagate’s ambitious goal is to ship to real-time changes in demand—not to plans or forecasts.
Responding to customer requests used to take about a week. Now it takes about a day.

Maturity Model
